description: Lock Military Veterinary Corps to late Roman military animal-care specialization, not Achaemenid road stations or human-infirmary supply stores.

checks.reason: The node remains grounded in prior horse-care and veterinary medicine traditions.
Earlier veterinary practice is a historical foundation, not a one-to-one institutional requirement.
Late Roman military animal transport belongs with Roman cursus-publicus road-station infrastructure.
Roman road waystations provide enabling infrastructure for movement and communications, not a strict veterinary-method requirement.
Achaemenid royal-road waystations must not carry the late Roman military-veterinary scope.
A military veterinarian is an animal-care specialization inside a broader military medical context.
Military field medicine supplies the organized medical-specialty context without making human field medicine a one-to-one veterinary method.
Roman human-infirmary supply stores are too narrow to carry the direct veterinary-corps prerequisite.
The node remains anchored to late Roman/Constantinian military veterinary evidence, not to a modern formal veterinary corps.
